gaius_marius_convo_1_n01.npcDialogue: The senate gave Sulla my command. Handed it over like a father passing a sword to his favorite son — never mind the old man who forged it. But Sulpicius — Sulpicius has spine. The tribal vote is in three days. Three days, and then Sulla chooses: bow to the people or draw his sword against them. I need to know something from you before that hour comes, and I need to know it now.

options.playerDialogue: I'm with you, Gaius Marius. Whatever comes next.
I'm with you — but what happens when Sulla says no? Because he will say no.
And if the vote fails? What then?

gaius_marius_convo_1_n02.npcDialogue: Good. That's the only answer that means anything — the one given before the blood starts. I've watched men pledge loyalty with the battle already won and call themselves brave. You said it now, in the quiet, when it could still cost you everything. I won't forget that.

options.playerDialogue: Then use me. What do you need?

gaius_marius_convo_1_n03.npcDialogue: When. When Sulla refuses. Don't soften it — I like that you don't soften it. He'll march from Capua with six legions and the gods know what he tells himself to sleep at night. I've known this was coming since the senate vote. The question isn't whether he does it — the question is whether Rome survives it. I broke the Cimbri at Vercellae. I broke Jugurtha in the desert. I can break one vain patrician who thinks a good surname is the same as a good sword arm.

options.playerDialogue: Then we prepare now, while there's still time. What's the plan?

gaius_marius_convo_1_n04.npcDialogue: The vote won't fail. I've counted the tribes myself — the old way, one by one, in the tabernae where the real politics happen. But you're right to ask. A general who doesn't plan for the spear in his back deserves to feel it. So tell me — what kind of man are you when everything falls apart?

responses.playerDialogue: I hold the line. The plan can burn — discipline doesn't.
I talk. There's always a door if you're willing to look for it.
I attack. When the plan dies, speed is the only thing left.
I adapt. The strongest man on the field isn't the one who hits hardest — he's the one who reads the ground.

fallbackResponse.playerDialogue: I survive. I do what the moment demands.

gaius_marius_convo_1_n05.npcDialogue: Listen to me carefully. Sulla has officers billeted across the city — his men, loyal to him, not to Rome, not to the Republic, to him personally. When the vote passes they'll move to obstruct. I want them contained. Not killed — I am not a butcher, whatever the senate whispers. Detained. Quietly. Can you do that with the men you've been cultivating?

options.playerDialogue: Twenty men at each billet point by dawn. Give me the addresses and consider it done.
Seizing officers without trial — that's Sulla's method, not yours. There has to be another way.

gaius_marius_convo_1_n06.npcDialogue: Principles. I had those once. They were beautiful — polished like marble, useless as marble when the swords come out. But I understand the need to hold something clean.

gaius_marius_convo_1_n07.npcDialogue: Some of them got out. Three officers, maybe four — they'll be at Capua by tomorrow morning, whispering in Sulla's ear. So now it's faster than I wanted. He'll march. He'll march and he'll tell himself the Republic made him do it. The only question left is how much blood and whose. I'm leaving Rome tonight. You can come with me or you can stay.

options.playerDialogue: I'll stay. Someone has to hold the city together while you're gone.
I'm coming. Wherever this road goes, I want to be on it.

gaius_marius_convo_1_n08.npcDialogue: Done. Every last one of them, penned up like sheep before the Kalends. The vote passes tomorrow and for one shining moment the Republic works exactly the way the founders imagined. Savor it. It won't last. It never lasts. Sulla will march, and when he does, the world changes.

options.playerDialogue: When his legions reach the walls — what then?

gaius_marius_convo_1_n09.npcDialogue: Then get out of my sight. Go polish your principles in some quiet corner while men like me and Sulla decide whether the world burns or bends. And when you're sitting in whatever exile your caution buys you — some fishing village, some Greek backwater — remember that Gaius Marius offered you a place at his table and you chose the floor.

gaius_marius_convo_1_n10.npcDialogue: Stay, then. Hold the forum. Hold it with your teeth if you have to. Keep Sulpicius alive — they'll come for him first, the optimates always kill the voice before the body. When I come back I will need Rome to still be something worth saving. Even a ruin. Even a shell. Can you give me that?

gaius_marius_convo_1_n11.npcDialogue: Pack light. Where we're going you won't need a toga — just good boots and a strong stomach. If we live through the next month, you'll stand beside the man who saved the Republic twice. And if we don't — well. I've seen worse deaths than dying next to someone who chose to be there.

deltas.reason: Marius entrusted you with holding Rome
Sulla's agents identify you as a Marian loyalist in the city
Chose duty in Rome over glory in the field
Bound your fate to Marius in the field
You are now an enemy combatant in Sulla's eyes
The senate considers you a rebel
Chose the march when staying was safer
Marius considers you a deserter from the cause
Preserved yourself when commitment meant danger
